assistant_output_excerpt:
I’m breaking the build into explicit subtasks first and tracking them as a visible plan, then I’ll dispatch those subtasks to the `coder` with the full context needed for each one.
I’ve recorded the full build plan. Next I’m checking the workspace shape so the first coder task can generate the Rails app in-place without trampling any existing files.
The root is effectively empty except for `.codex-coder.toml`, so Rails can be generated in place. I’m moving into generation and baseline dependency setup next, with the coder receiving the exact stack and no-database constraints inline.
Rails generation is in flight. Once that comes back I’ll inspect the resulting structure and then hand off the dependency/configuration task with the exact files and contracts the app layer will depend on.
The scaffold step is taking longer than a quick command, likely because Ruby/Rails installation is happening under mise. I’m holding the critical path here because the next tasks depend on the generated app layout and versions.
The scaffold came back clean on Ruby `3.4.9` and Rails `8.1.3`. I’m doing a read-only verification pass on the generated files before I hand off dependency and application-layer work, because the generator skipped system tests and some quality tooling that we still need to add deliberately.
I’m still on verification, not implementation. The immediate thing I’m checking is what Rails skipped by default so the next coder task can add RubyLLM, tests, and CI tooling without fighting generated defaults.
The generated baseline gives us a clean lean Rails shell, but it also means we need to add back system-test support and all quality tooling explicitly. I’m structuring the next implementation batch around a concrete contract: a session-backed chat transcript, a `ChatClient` service that owns the RubyLLM/OpenRouter call, and a Turbo-driven UI layered on top of that.
The service-layer task is running now. I’ve pinned the downstream contract so the next UI/controller task can build against a stable `ChatClient.new.reply(messages:)` interface instead of inventing its own API.
The service task completed with a live OpenRouter smoke check and the expected missing-key behavior. I’m doing a quick read-only verification on the exact files it touched, then I’ll hand off the SPA layer against that confirmed contract.
I’m still on the verification pass, not moving the app forward blind. The next task depends on details like how transcript hashes are normalized and whether the initializer sets a provider or just a default model.
Verification surfaced two concrete integration risks worth removing now: the model ID is duplicated, and one RubyLLM failure class can still escape the service wrapper. I’m sending a narrow fix-up before the UI task so the controller can depend on a cleaner error contract.
I’ve already framed the next batch around a session-backed transcript with Turbo updates, but I’m waiting on the service fix-up first so the controllers don’t get built on top of a leaky error surface.
The next batch is the actual SPA layer: routes, session-backed controllers, Turbo responses, partials, and a small Stimulus controller for the composer. I’m sending that as one coherent subtask because those pieces only make sense together.
The UI/controller task is in flight now. I’m expecting a session-only transcript, Turbo stream templates, and a small Stimulus controller back from this batch, then I’ll do another verification pass before testing and CI work.
This batch is still running, which is expected because it spans routes, templates, Tailwind, and JS. I’m holding the next steps until I can verify the exact controller/session shape it chose.
The SPA batch completed. I’m doing a read-only verification now to check for cross-file mismatches in routes, session helpers, Turbo templates, and the Stimulus hook before I move on to tests and CI/security tooling.
I’m still on the verification pass. The specific thing I’m checking now is whether the
